sourceFiles.notes: Fake sample source for schema validation only.

vocabulary.studentDefinition: Velocity tells how fast something moves and which direction it moves.
vocabulary.teacherDefinition: Velocity is a vector quantity describing displacement per unit time.
vocabulary.misconception: Students may treat velocity as identical to speed and leave out direction.
vocabulary.exampleQuestion: A cart moves east at 3 m/s. What is its velocity?

vocabulary.studentDefinition: Net force is the overall force after forces are combined.
vocabulary.teacherDefinition: Net force is the vector sum of all forces acting on an object.
vocabulary.misconception: Students may add opposite-direction forces without subtracting.
vocabulary.exampleQuestion: A box has 8 N right and 3 N left. What is the net force?
vocabulary.exampleAnswer: The net force is 5 N right.
vocabulary.sourceTextSnippet: Net force is the combination of all forces on an object.

concepts.title: Balanced and Unbalanced Forces
concepts.studentExplanation: Balanced forces cancel out. Unbalanced forces do not cancel out and can change motion.
keyIdeas: Balanced forces have a net force of 0 N.
Unbalanced forces can speed up, slow down, or turn an object.
examples: Two equal pushes in opposite directions are balanced.
nonExamples: A 10 N push right and a 4 N push left are not balanced.
commonMisconceptions: An object must be still for forces to be balanced.
concepts.sourceTextSnippet: Balanced forces produce no change in motion.

concepts.title: Reading Simple Motion Graphs
concepts.studentExplanation: A motion graph shows how an object's motion changes over time.
keyIdeas: A steeper line can mean faster motion.
A flat position-time line means position is not changing.
examples: A rising position-time line shows movement away from the start.
nonExamples: A graph title alone does not tell the full motion story.
commonMisconceptions: Students may think every upward line means going uphill.
concepts.sourceTextSnippet: Slope on a position-time graph describes speed.

referenceFormulas.title: Average Speed
referenceFormulas.equation: average speed = total distance / total time

variables.meaning: how fast something moves on average

referenceFormulas.studentExplanation: Average speed compares the total distance traveled with the total time.
referenceFormulas.sourceTextSnippet: Average speed is total distance divided by total time.

problemBank.question: A box is pushed with 6 N right and 6 N left. Are the forces balanced?
problemBank.expectedAnswer: Yes. The forces are balanced because the net force is 0 N.

problemBank.question: On a position-time graph, what can a steeper line mean?
problemBank.expectedAnswer: A steeper line can mean the object is moving faster.

standardsMap.description: Describe how balanced and unbalanced forces affect motion.
Interpret simple representations of object motion.

smokeTests.question: What is net force?
smokeTests.expectedAnswer: Net force is the overall force after forces are combined.

smokeTests.question: Can this uploaded average speed formula solve problems automatically?
